experiments.title: How testing different Dear Reader survey topics impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a dear reader asking for opinions on the polarizing topic of the border crisis in Texas for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because readers will be more drawn to that topic than of school choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF has two active surveys: School Choice and Border Crisis. We want to see which survey topic is most appealing to our reader audience and can collect the most emails, so we’ve separated them via a 50/50 Dear Reader experiment.
These Dear Readers are implemented at the end of policy issue articles, so 50% of readers will be asked to complete the School Choice Survey and 50% will be asked to complete the Border Crisis Survey once they are done reading their respective article.
Let’s see which survey is most appealing to our audience and acquires the most emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The implementation of a "dear reader" treatment asking for opinions on the polarizing topic of the border crisis in Texas resulted in a significant increase (99.4% level of confidence) in email acquisition. The treatment group experienced an 80.2% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group.
The treatment also resulted in one downstream donation compared to none to the control.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the topic of the border crisis in Texas is highly relevant and engaging for page visitors, more so than the topic of School Choice. This suggests that future experimentation should continue to explore this topic and its potential impact on user engagement and email acquisition, and that this org should roll out the Border Crisis Dear Reader to 100% of traffic.
It is also worth noting that the experiment ran for 13 days, which may not capture all potential long-term effects. Future experiments could consider running for a longer duration to assess any sustained impact on email acquisition.
In summary, the key learnings from this experiment emphasize the importance of selecting relevant and engaging topics to drive user engagement and email acquisition.

				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic

experiments.title: How testing different Dear Reader survey topics impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
implementing a dear reader asking for opinions on the active topic of the top issues in Texas for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because readers will be more drawn to that topic than of school choice.
because  readers will be more drawn to that topic than of school choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2023 - 06/09/2023
TPPF has two active surveys: Top Issues in Texas and School Choice. We want to see which survey topic is most appealing to our reader audience and can collect the most emails, so we’ve separated them via a 50/50 Dear Reader experiment.
These Dear Readers are implemented at the end of policy issue articles, so 50% of readers will be asked to complete the Top Issues in Texas Survey and 50% will be asked to complete the School Choice Survey once they are done reading their respective article.
Let’s see which version acquires the most emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Dear Reader pointing readers to our Top Issues in Texas did not perform as well as its counterpart - the School Choice survey. In fact, the Top Issues in Texas variant produced a 41.3% decrease in email conversion rate with a valid 98.7% level of confidence.
The results give us valid proof that more page visitors were interested in offering their opinions on a specific topic rather than a more general topic. School choice is getting a lot of headlines in Texas right now so it could be that the timing was right for directing readers to a school choice survey. We'll have to monitor the news flow to see if the School Choice survey holds up as a Dear Reader or if we need to put a new survey up against it to see if a new topic resonates better with our readers.
The value proposition for each Dear Reader followed a similar template, so without a different approach in value proposition, the results really do boil down to the topic presented. We will roll out the School Choice Survey to 100% of our policy issues reader audience.
It's important to test different dear readers to see which resonates best with our readers and deserves the long standing spot for our standard policy research dear readers. We will continue to test to determine which one has the greatest potential to rack up emails and enhance engagement.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly giving donation tab for a recurring giving ask impacted recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting the monthly giving donation form tab as the default tab  for potential donors  will achieve an increase in recurring giving because the monthly giving tab will align with donation ask in the page copy, maintaining the monthly giving momentum and thought process, and reducing donor friction. because  the monthly giving tab will align with donation ask in the page copy, maintaining the monthly giving momentum and thought process, and reducing donor friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2023 - 02/18/2023
A public policy client asks users to make a monthly donation and join their monthly donor program after signing a petition. The ask details the monthly donor program, highlighting its purpose and the benefits of joining. But when the petition signers reach the donation form, the form defaults to a one-time gift ask. The form consists of two tabs, one for making a one-time gift and one for making a monthly gift to “Join the Come and Take It Society” – as the tab is labeled.
We wondered if the misalignment of making a recurring giving ask but defaulting the tabbed form to a one-time ask was causing friction, disrupting the user momentum, and possibly even causing confusion among potential donors. To test this line of thinking, we simply built a treatment that set the “Join the Come and Take It Society” tab as the default. Would this tweak result in an increase in monthly donors?
It’s also important to note that the monthly giving tab is supported by a monthly giving callout which reiterates some of the value proposition from the page copy and isolates several benefits of joining the Come and Take It Society.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a valid increase in recurring giving, producing four recurring gifts to the treatment and none to the control. This validates our hypothesis that potential donors benefit from congruency between the recurring donation ask and the recurring giving donation form tab. It makes sense for the recurring ask to be backed by a recurring giving opportunity through the donation form. It is also important for potential donors to see and read the recurring giving callout as it could very well add motivation for them to convert as a recurring donor. If the donation form defaults to one-time giving then there is a strong chance the user will not click to the recurring tab and they would therefore never have the chance to read the callout.
This test is worth testing on additional offers that include a recurring giving ask at the end of the funnel.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page for monthly partnership impacted donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conducting a radical redesign on a monthly donor membership donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in instant donor conversion rate because potential donors will gain clarity on the benefits of joining the membership club and will have a cleaner, more user-friendly page engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/05/2022
A public policy client has a special membership club for monthly donors. Someone can join this club when making a monthly donation of at least $10, and from there they’ll receive special perks and have an elevated impact on the organization and its cause. The donation page where someone can make a monthly gift to join the club is resulting in a low instant conversion rate. Considering the amount of traffic that reaches the page, and after brainstorming several potential ways to improve conversion, we’ve opted for a radical redesign test to determine its impact on the instant donor conversion rate.
We will test these elements:
Expanding the value proposition of the headline
Moving the inline quote outside of the main copy and into a quote box on the side
Calling out the specific impact of making a monthly gift via bullet points
Moving the (501(c)3) tax language to the bottom of the page below the giving button
Setting the default gift array amount to $10
Changing “Donor Information” to “Your Information” in the giving form
Removing an email subscription opportunity from above the giving button
Removing only on mobile the social proof slide in informing the page visitor that [someone] just joined the membership club
We want to determine if this combination of changes will improve the page/donation experience for the visitor, provide additional clarity on the benefits of joining the membership club, and ultimately increase donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results are in. The treatment (radical redesign) showed a valid 76.5% DECREASE in instant donor conversion rate. The control variant produced 8 conversions to the treatment's 2.
Our swing for the fences resulted in a whiff. The results were negative across segments. Unfortunately, a radical redesign cannot tell us how each change influenced the user experience and donor conversion rate overall. To simplify this test and make more sense of the results, our next test should isolate one or two variants. We may want to go down the list of changes so we can truly determine which change(s) turned potential donors off.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 76.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a radical redesign affected donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign  for potential monthly donors will achieve an increased conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2020 - 11/08/2020
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate for a monthly donation ask, we developed a treatment for the donation page with new copy, new graphics, and an increased monthly ask amount. We added exclusivity to the headline, increased clarity in the descriptions of the benefits for joining as a monthly donor, and relevance to the intro paragraphs. We also made the photo on the page more congruent with the ad they had seen before arriving on this page and increased the monthly gift ask from $10 to $15. We wanted to see how this would impact the overall conversion rate as well as average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we saw differing responses from individual segments, overall we saw a 31.6% decrease in conversion rate and turned the experiment off. What we would like to explore next is isolating the copy, image, and monthly gift ask to identify which stand-alone element, if any, was responsible for the decrease. Rather than taking these results at face value, we also need to evaluate if the increase in average gift potentially reflects a greater value than the decrease in conversions to see which is a more beneficial long-term approach. We did not reach statistical significance to have valid results for the increase in average gift to determine its effect on increasing revenue.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 40.2% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 44.8% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 31.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How lower grade level body copy readability impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the work required to read through a donation page for acquisition offer visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2021 - 01/16/2022
On a new acquisition offer, we wanted to test using more straight-foward copy that was brief and easy to read compared to traditionally policy-focused and academic copy on an instant donation page. The treatment copy read at a 7th grade level while the control was rated a 14th grade reading level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the treatment page with the 7th-grade reading level copy decreased conversion by 60% with 98% confidence. The control copy was more convincing that there was a problem and that it could be solved through the donor's support. For future testing, we will need to reevaluate how to keep the strength of the messaging while also altering the copy to be easier to read.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 48.6% decrease with a 87% level of confidence.
(Other) visitors had a 53.2% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 78.0% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 53.4% decrease in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 71.8% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How humanizing a subject line impacted open rates on an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking a question in the subject line for email recipients will achieve an increased open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/19/2020
TPPF was sending a monthly appeal reminder for a matching gift deadline. The initial appeal was sent a week prior and this message was from another team member following up. We wanted to test if including a question in the subject line calling attention to the previous email would increase the open rate by coming across as more human rather than indicating urgency.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment ended up increasing both open rate and clickthrough rate for the email. We saw a 10.6% lift in open rate with 98.3% confidence. Even though it is a limited tactic since we know this approach will not work for every email the organization sends, we can use this finding to be strategic about highlighting certain emails or appeals in the future that we need to draw extra attention to.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image for ad visitors would increase click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/09/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition was experiencing a low click-through rate, so we tested a few different ad angles to see if they would increase clicks. We wondered if grabbing the reader’s attention right off the bat with a statement that they could “get behind” and clearly understand what was at stake would increase click-through rate. We also wondered if the existing ad image was experiencing user fatigue, and therefore tested two new images to see how they impact click-through rate. We ran an a/b/c test. The copy for the “b” and “c” variants differs from the “a” variant only in the opening sentence of the copy.
The “b” variant has a different image than “a”, using an image with symbols that represent Texas and that almost any Texan would recognize. The “c” variant has a different image as well, showing a person riding a horse in open Texas land – an image that we hypothesized Texans would relate to and distinguish themselves through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatment 1 and 2 led to an increase in ad clicks. Treatment 1 saw a 38.9% lift in clicks with a 96.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 83.4% lift in clicks with 99.9% level of confidence. This tells us that users did appreciate the clarity in the opening line of the ad copy, as they learned immediately what the ad was asking of them (to sign a petition) and what the petition was for (to keep Texas Texan). Also, by pointing at this this petition was "critical", users likely felt more of a sense of urgency to click the ad to reach the critical petition. As these variables were not isolated, we don't know for sure whether the clarity or urgency factored more into their decision to click through to the landing page.

Ultimately, treatment 2 experienced the biggest life of the three variants. This tells us that the ad image in treatment 2 resonated the most with users. They're asked to complete a petition to "keep Texas Texan", and the image of a man riding a horse through an open field on a dirt path rightly represents Texas. Users were more likely to click an ad image that offered authenticity than an image that was more "cartoony". That's because users see Texas and Texans as "authentic".

This ad approach is worth testing with other ads and for other offers. The results and learning would be clearer, however, if the ad variants are isolated. We don't know for sure if users were more motivated to click because of the copy change or the image change.

experiments.title: How an “us vs. them” copy angle on a petition landing page impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an “us” vs. “them” copy angle in which we isolate the opposition and unite against the opposition for potential petition signers will achieve an increase in sign up rate and conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/02/2022 - 10/01/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is designed to build momentum towards a free and independent Texas, calling out important fundamental beliefs among Texans and asking people to stand behind them by signing the petition. The petition landing page was experiencing a low sign up rate, and we wondered if the lead in copy to the petition was lacking motivation.
We tested an “us vs. them” copy angle against the control copy which was more organization-centric and focused on the importance of keeping Texas true to its values as more and more people migrate to the state. For the treatment, the “us” is loyal conservative Texans and the “them” is politicians, policymakers, and progressives working against “us”. We thought calling out the “bad guy” and stating how we’re united against the “bad guy” would enhance motivation and convince more people to sign the petition. We also thought the motivation would carry over to the instant donation page and have a positive impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Sign ups (email acqusition) showed little difference between the control and treatment; the treatment saw a 2.3% decrease in sign ups with only a 50.7% level of confidence. However, the treatment saw a valid 74.4% decrease in donations. 11 donations were made to the control compared to only 3 to the treatment. This tells us that any motivation created from the "us" vs. "them" copy angle did not carry over to the donation page.
Interestingly, mobile users made all 11 donations to the control and the treatment actually saw more donations among desktop users. Mobile also had far more samples than desktop. This indicates that mobile users may not have been turned off by the "us" vs. "them" copy angle, but that they preferred briefer copy as they were reaching the actual petition. This experiment may be worth testing again, but with a briefer version of the treatment. If we do retest and the control wins again, then it would be more clear that the control copy angle is more impactful than the treatment copy angle.

0% increase in traffic
				× 79.2% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How a video affects click-through rates when compared to a survey preview for a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a video ad will increase click rates on a Facebook ad for a survey offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
Recently we have seen success using video format ads and survey preview ads, so naturally, we wanted to test them against each other to see how they would perform head-to-head.
For this experiment, we pitted a new video treatment against our survey preview control, left all other ad elements unchanged, and ran the two ads for 30 days to compare results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 30 days, the results of our test were conclusive, with the video ad producing a 77.6% decline in click-through rates when compared with the survey preview, with a 100% level of confidence.
This further validates our past learnings that previewing a survey in the ad creative is likely to increase click rates by catching the user's attention and engaging them visually on an issue they care about, leading them forward into the offer where they want to share their thoughts.
We'll now look to globalize this across survey offers for our other public policy clients.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and relevancy to a subject line on an appeal impacted open rates

rows.name: A critical year for the future of freedom for Texans

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy and urgency for housefile recipients of a digital appeal will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2021 - 02/22/2021
When sending a monthly appeal, we wanted to test if adding urgency and relevancy in the subject line would influence open rates. The control subject line “This legislative session” contained an element of mystery but we developed a treatment subject line “A critical year for the future of freedom for Texans” to draw attention to the timeliness and to make a personal connection to the reader, especially if they are a Texan concerned about freedom.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the subject line with additional detail reduced opens by 36.3% while the succinct, mysterious subject line outperformed the treatment with 100% level of confidence. One hypothesis is that "A critical year" indicates the recipient could take action at any time during the year versus "This legislative session" has a more finite timeframe thereby making it more relevant and timely. The element of mystery proved to drive open rates more than clarity in this specific instance. We'll use this finding to develop additional subject line tests in the future.

experiments.title: How including the first survey question in an email impacted clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the first question of a survey for a housefile email will achieve more clicks to the survey page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/11/2021
We hypothesized that adding the first question of a survey to give people a preview/example of the questions they would be answering and highlighting an easy entry point to begin their survey that we could increase momentum from the email and get more clicks to the survey page. By featuring one of the questions we provide clarity to the reader about what the survey process looks like and decrease anxiety and friction about the amount of time and work required to complete the survey. We also introduce an opportunity to exercise the concept of a “micro-yes” – once people are nodding their heads and saying yes to answering the first question, we can help them continue saying yes throughout the survey and eventually on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment with the first question included in the body copy of the email generated a 59.5% increase in traffic with 99.9% level of confidence. Looking further down the process, we also saw a 40% lift in the number of emails acquired through the treatment with a 97.1% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign with simplified copy impacted a housefile digital appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language for a housefile appeal email will achieve an increase in engagement.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/11/2021
As a part of the special legislative session, Texas Public Policy Foundation made an appeal around their proposed plan to lower property taxes.
The digital appeals to the housefile had been losing steam to convert donors in recent months, so they decided to test the language of the appeal and see what they could learn about the audience response.
The original email reflected a lot of policy-specific language and when graded in a readability tool, it was projected to be at a 12th grade reading level. So they developed a treatment that dramatically simplified the language and presented at a 5th grade reading level.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the simplified version of the email led to a 54.5% increase in the overall open rate on the email. Diving in deeper, we were not able to secure enough traffic to validate the clickthrough rate but the treatment had a 16% increase on clicks with a 52% level of confidence. This learning can certainly be replicated and applied for future digital appeals, but can also be tested in the acquisition space to see how prospective audiences respond to simplified language compared to the housefile. We can also identify a few donation pages where we may be able to test this simplified language concept.

experiments.title: How asking a specific question in an ad impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplified Facebook ads posing a relevant question for potential donors will achieve an increase in traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/27/2021
As part of a direct ask campaign for monthly donations, the Texas Public Policy Foundation wanted to test if specific policy ads would outperform a general invitation to join a branded recurring donor program. They had seen how other organizations had seen increases in traffic conversion through simplifying ads and wanted to test the concept on their images for the policy-specific ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed a 1,223.4% lift in click rate on these Facebook ads that included a banner with the option to select "Yes" or "No" to indicate if they align with the organization's stance on specific policy areas. Once the visitors clicked on the call to take action, we were able to present them with the recurring donation ask to support TPPF's efforts in that specific policy area they just told us they have a shared belief.
What's interesting is that when we looked at our donor conversion rate on the donation page,  we actually saw an 80.5% decrease with 100% Level of Confidence—which means that now that we've determined how to increase the number of clicks in order to drive a substantial amount traffic to the page, we can now shift our focus to running experiments to improve conversion on the donation page.
While the increase in traffic is not necessarily producing visits from highly motivated donors to the page, we still see this as a worthwhile effort because once they visit the donation page we have the ability to set up additional campaigns designed to retarget them and find other opportunities to reach them distinctly with content related to their policy interests.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
1,223.4% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How using a question in a subject line impacted open rate for an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a conversational question in a subject line impact the open rate of an email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
TPPF was re-sending a matching gift appeal from a new sender and wanted to test whether using a question in the subject line would increase open rate on the email. The original appeal with the matching gift details had been sent from the organization’s executive director two weeks prior, so we decided to send a follow-up message from the development team calling attention to the previous email. The control subject line “Deadline today” contained a sense of urgency and mystery while the treatment “Did you see Kevin’s email?” utilized asking a direct, relevant question and also came across using a more humanized and personal approach.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line with the question had an increase in open rate leading to a 10% lift in traffic when compared to the control. The treatment also saw an increase from 1.41% to 1.5% in the overall clickthrough rate. While we know this approach cannot be used for each email the organization sends, since the experiment results are valid, we can use these findings to strategically highlight especially significant emails to the housefile in the future to gain attention in the inbox and increase open and clickthrough rates.
