experiments.title: How increasing urgency and perceived need affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing urgency and perceived need affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2018 - 01/13/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a campaign to raise support for their Center for the American Future, which defended citizens against government overreach. They were running a petition of support for John Yearwood, a rancher who faced a threat from the government if he disturbed the habitat of a rare spider—one which he had never seen!
The petition was followed by a donation ask, which was not performing very well. They decided to create a treatment that redesigned the page and message to present the need and urgency in a more forceful manner, hypothesizing that it would increase donor conversion. The treatment page added a headline that thanked the signer. They then tried to heighten the stakes by telling the reader that the stakes of this case were high, and could possibly affect them. The next paragraph of copy focused on a single fact—Mr. Yearwood can’t fund this case by himself, and hundreds of Texans are stepping up to help him fund it. Then comes the “but” statement—they are currently short of goal. They put a thermometer on the page that showed how close they were, and provided some social proof.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation page produced a 236.1% increase in donor conversion, which showed the importance of communicating not just the cause, but the need for funds. Additionally, social proof and urgency—in this instance, the case moving forward without the necessary funds had an effect.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 236.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How speaking directly to the donor affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will speaking directly to the donor affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2019 - 06/10/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a property tax survey for Texans, followed by a donation ask. The campaign was acquiring donors at a decent rate, but with the volume of people taking the survey, they thought they could convert more. When they looked at the donation page, they noticed that the first question was a “grand statement”, followed by a bulleted list of groups that are affected by property taxes. Also, the text asked them to be a part of a “growing movement”—and previous research had shown that when prospective TPPF donors were shown language about being a part of a larger group, it reduced results. The TPPF team thought that the readers might not empathize with every group listed here, and that a more direct, personal approach might convert more donors. They created a treatment that spoke directly to the reader, only addressing the needs that they knew a survey-taker would have: home ownership, personal tax burden, and cost of living.
They launched a test between the two pages to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With similar amounts of traffic, the treatment produced 85.3% more donations. This reinforced the fact that TPPF donors are more concerned with personal impact than the impact of groups, and showed the power of speaking in a "one-to-one" manner.
Once the winner is rolled out, the results on the campaign will drastically improve thanks to this experiment.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 85.3% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How “shared responsibility” affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How will removing “shared responsibility” to highlight personal responsibility affect response rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was promoting a property tax survey, which was followed by a donation ask. The original version of the Facebook ad asked the reader to “join your fellow Texans” and take the survey. However, they wondered if this “shared responsibility” might actually be negatively affecting clickthrough rate. The reader might think that there are plenty of other people taking the survey, and not feel inclined to complete it personally. They removed that line and launched a test to see if they could improve response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The second ad, which removed the "shared responsibility" sentence from the copy produced a 25% increase in clickthrough rate, which led to a large increase in both names acquired and donations. A single sentence might not presume to have so much impact, but considering the audience, personal responsibility is a big motivator. This learning can be used in future offers to optimize results.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
25.0% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How validating shared beliefs on a petition affects downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will validating shared beliefs on a petition affects downstream donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a campaign to raise support for their Center for the American Future, which defended citizens against government overreach. They were running a petition of support for John Yearwood, a rancher who faced a threat from the government if he disturbed the habitat of a rare spider—one which he had never seen!
They ran an experiment on the petition page to see if adding a section that asked the reader to confirm their shared values—namely that Texas landowners should be able to use their land as they please, that the federal government should let property owners conserve their own land, and that the government should follow the 10th Amendment—would increase donor conversion. Previous testing had implied that these checkboxes could have an impact on email acquisition, but were more likely to have an impact on donations (more people would give after signing). They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the checkboxes treatment had no effect on email acquisition, it cut downstream conversion by half. The hypothesis as to why is that "zooming out" to shared beliefs took the focus off of the story, which made the ask (which was hyper-relevant to Yearwood) less congruent with the landing page message.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 52.2% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a content offer instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s School Choice survey is producing a low instant donor conversion rate once users complete the survey and are redirected to an instant donation page. In an effort to increase donation conversion rate, we’ve built a treatment that leverages a premium offer when someone makes a donation of at least $10.
The premium is TPPF’s Pocket Constitution. We’ve seen past success when offering the Pocket Constitution as a free offer, so we want to see if using the content offer as a premium in this instance proves to be successful through an increase in donations.
The only difference between the control and treatment donation page is the closing bold paragraph in the treatment that informs page visitors of the premium opportunity and incudes an image of the premium to the right of the paragraph for visual appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, the treatment produced a 100% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 84.5% level of confidence. The treatment produced zero donations compared to two to the control.

Despite the nearly valid level of confidence, the outcome of this experiment should be considered lightly due to a total of only two transactions. The recommended number of transactions to confidently review outcomes is 50, so these results are well below that bar.

The traffic sources to this experiment was limited to only a Dear Reader, as we turned the School Choice offer off in paid social due to consistently low performance. Thus, this experiment did not benefit from the healthy amount of traffic it needed to generate more transactions.

The treatment would be worth testing again on another more popular/active offer to gain a better idea of the effectiveness of the premium concept and the specific premium offer.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain clarity and motivation as to how serious the problem is that we’re asking them to help solve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/10/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis Survey instant donation page is getting an instant conversion rate below 1%. In brainstorming ways to potentially increase conversion rate, we wondered if the addition of evidentials would further clarify and intensify the problem that the donor’s gift would help solve. For a test treatment, we added three bullet points towards the beginning of the value proposition copy, each one stating a research fact that speaks to the problems at the border.
Will statistics and facts lead to more clarity and motivation, and ultimately more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy may not necessarily increase donations. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. The treatment resulted in a 52.3% decrease in conversions with a statistical level of confidence of 83.2%. These results suggest that potential donors may not be motivated by research facts and statistics. We can also surmise that donors already have a strong understanding of the problem and that they don't need evidentials to help with their understanding.

As the problem is clearly known and communicated throughout the offer funnel, future tests need to focus on other components of the instant donation page, such as the solutions or the ask. Potential donors see eye to eye with the organization on the problem, but maybe they are not on the same page with the solutions we are offering, or maybe the solutions are not clear enough.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain clarity and motivation as to how serious the problem is that we’re asking them to help solve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/10/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis Survey instant donation page is getting an instant conversion rate below 1%. In brainstorming ways to potentially increase conversion rate, we wondered if the addition of evidentials would further clarify and intensify the problem that the donor’s gift would help solve. For a test treatment, we added three bullet points towards the beginning of the value proposition copy, each one stating a research fact that speaks to the problems at the border.
Will statistics and facts lead to more clarity and motivation, and ultimately more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy may not necessarily increase donations. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. The treatment resulted in a 52.3% decrease in conversions with a statistical level of confidence of 83.2%. These results suggest that potential donors may not be motivated by research facts and statistics. We can also surmise that donors already have a strong understanding of the problem and that they don't need evidentials to help with their understanding.

As the problem is clearly known and communicated, future tests need to focus on other components of the instant donation page, such as the solutions or the ask. Potential donors see eye to eye with the organization on the problem, but maybe they are not on the same page with the solutions we are offering.

experiments.title: How adding first name personalization to the headline of an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding first name personalization to the headline of an instant donation page for page visitors will build trust and relations between the potential donor and the organization, and will ultimately achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2022 - 11/07/2022
For TPPF’s newest offer, the Top Issues in Texas survey, we were interested to see how users would take to first name personalization in the headline of the offer of instant donation page. Once a user submits the survey by providing their first name, last name, and email address, they would be redirected to the instant donation page where the headline would refer to them by their first name.
We wondered if this personalization method would make the user feel acknowledged, and build trust and relations with the visitor, leading them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment validated with the treatment resulting in an 86.3% DECREASE in donor conversion rate (95.7% level of confidence). There was a significant decrease across all segments, revealing that users either lost motivation to give or did not gain motivation to give when they were acknowledged by their first name when reaching the instant donation page. This may be an audience that is suspicious of seeing their first name when they do not have a personal relationship with the organization. This experiment may be worth testing with another offer to determine if the TPPF audience overall is turned off by first name personalization, or if the response is specific to the audience for this offer.

experiments.title: How previewing the survey affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a survey for FB users will achieve higher click-through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2020 - 07/09/2022
In an effort to improve click-through-rates for a FB ad pointing people to a survey on the Border Crisis, we wanted to test if a radical redesign centered on previewing the survey, rather than highlighting the problem, would inspire more viewers to click. For this experiment, we mocked up a survey question on the ad image so that the viewer would be prompted to click to select their answer
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 25.6% lift in clicks with an 84% level of confidence. These results lead us to believe that by previewing the survey and thus focusing on the solution in the form of an action, rather than the problem, more viewers were inspired to act.

While these results are promising, because this experiment altered several elements of the ad: previewing the survey in the ad image + shortening the body text to focus on the desired action, we can not say with certainty which change had more of an impact on this lift.

Our hunch is that the image, being more visible when people scroll their feeds, played more of a role ... but to further validate these results, we will next want to test a new image using the same body copy as the winning ad above.

experiments.title: How removing the organization from a sender name affected click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization from the sender name for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2022
A new president was announced within the organization, meaning their email list would be receiving emails from a sender name they may not recognize. To mitigate confusion and prevent disengagement, unsubscribes, and SPAM complaints, the organization name was appended to the sender name on emails sent to their list. We wanted to know how this was affecting click rates and decided to test if we would see a lift in click rates by once again removing the organization from the sender name. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 101.5% lift in click rates, with an 84.7% level of confidence. Despite being unable to validate this lift, the results provide a strong indication that there may be a link between sender name and click rates. This is especially interesting because the sender name is most often thought to affect open rates, but its impact on click rates is less commonly documented.
These results reinforce the knowledge that the more personal and human your outreach (especially via email), the better chance you have of connecting with donors and inspiring them to act. Whenever possible, organizations should seek to humanize their outreach and appeals—this includes ensuring that emails are sent from real people within an organization and should appear in an inbox as much like a personal email as possible. The results also show how even a small change made to one part of a campaign can have a significant affect on downstream performance.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically redesigned donation page post-survey increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2020 - 07/09/2020
We wanted to increase donor conversion and thought the current donation page copy was causing a disjointed experience for the donor. We changed several elements to create a radical redesign test. Instead of leading with a “Success!” confirmation message upon survey submission, we wanted to draw the reader in further through personal appeal and direct continuation of the conversation. We also updated the copy with enhanced urgency and added a direct ask with a clear outcome “for a tax-deductible gift of $50, we’ll be able to inform 200 people about what’s at stake” rather than a general ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we hoped implementing a radical redesign would help us validate results, we did not reach a valid level of confidence and saw a directional decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis for the decrease is that the new copy did not draw as much attention to evidentials as the control. But for future tests, we will look into testing each variable—headline, evidential points, specific ask, and tone—individually so we can identify where the loss is incurred.

experiments.title: How optimizing Facebook for purchases impacts donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will Facebook’s standard “Purchase” event yield better donor acquisition for a petition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2018 - 12/14/2018
TPPF had been running their Facebook petition campaign for several weeks with the goal of acquiring additional donors for the organization. After nearly a month of activity, email acquisition had been happening at an extremely low cost but we were seeing exceptionally low instant donor conversion. After a discussion with our Facebook rep, we decided to test out Facebook’s native “Purchase” event to optimize our ads. They recommend that we have at least 50 conversions to optimize off of but we wanted to see if it would still be impactful with our low donor acquisition numbers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 5 days, we saw the Purchase event optimization had yielded three donors compared to the Control’s zero donors. This was the result of a 1.0% instant donor conversion rate for petition signers. Knowing the historical donor conversion rate for this campaign (0.1% instant donor conversion rate) this was a clear winner.
In the ideal world, we would have run this experiment for well over a month in order to see at least one purchase come in for the Control to validate the results. However, when our primary metric is to acquire donors and we are seeing significant improvement with the new method of optimization, it did not make sense to continue wasting our advertising budget just to validate an experiment.
It is worth noting that while the instant donor conversion rate jumped to 1% for petition signers, we also saw the email acquisition rate drop by 62% and our cost per email went up 48%. Both of these were successful tradeoffs given that we were finally able to acquire donors.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials in the donation page value proposition impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials through the value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the issue will feel closer to home for donation page visitors and they’ll be more inspired to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis instant donation page is producing a a low conversion rate. We wondered if enhancing the value proposition messaging through evidentials would better engage donation page visitors, as the messaging would feel closer to home and they’d therefore want to make a donation to help improve the border crisis situation.
The control uses more general value proposition while the treatment highlights specific threats to Texas citizens and examples of the harm the border crisis is causing to these citizens.
Let’s see which approach appeals more to our audience in terms of urging them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, the treatment resulted in a 45.2% decrease in donations with a 78.6% level of confidence. Potential donors did not respond to the treatment evidential-infused value proposition copy like we expected them to. Perhaps they already feel they have a strong grasp of the problem and therefore were not moved by the evidentials. In this case, we would want to hone in on the solution side of the formula more so than the problem side.
The treatment also produced a 29.5% decrease in revenue with a 73.6% level of confidence.
Returning visitors produced an 88% decrease in donations to the treatment with a valid 97.6% level of confidence - 1 donation to the treatment compared to 7 to the control. This provides further proof that our returning visitors already have a strong grasp of the problem.
Donors may care more about how their donation would make a difference in resolving the issues caused by the border crisis. Especially on this donation page where they're being asked to make a monthly donation, it's likely even more important to stress the reasons to give.
It would be interesting to run a test where we isolate the reasons to give between the control variant and the treatment variant to see how that impacts giving. In this instance, the treatment reasons to give focus more on stopping the bad guys (cartels) while the control reasons to focus more on implementing better policy. It would be interesting to isolate these to see which motivates our audience more to make donations.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is producing a low instant donor conversion rate after users sign the petition. In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their CEO’s picture and a message directing the reader’s attention to the donation opportunity below and reminding them of the impact that they can make. We believe this message coming from a person and prominent figure in the organization will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar with the picture of a prominent member of the organization and a personal message from that person can significantly increase the donor conversion rate - the power of personal touch. The treatment version of the donation page, which included the humanized sticky bar, saw a 164.8% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence is only 63.5% which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. This experiment had to end after 15 days, preventing the necessary data to reach validation. It also recorded only 4 total donations between the two variants, which is below the recommended 50 transactions to reach confident results.

experiments.title: How a donation exit intent popup on an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an exit intent popup prompting a return to the donation page will increase donations because potential donors will be brought back in by the ask and simplified value prop, and ultimately convert.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/17/2023
TPPF’s monthly donor conversion funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In an effort to increase conversion, we’ve implemented an exit intent popup that appears when someone tries to exit the page.
The popup will hit donation page visitors with one more ask with condensed value proposition copy in hopes that it will convince them to return to the donation page and complete a donation.
This approach could be effective in a number of ways:
It’s unconventional and it literally “pops out” to users
The popup will catch all users that try to exit, but could be especially impactful to the users that would bounce from the page immediately without reading the value proposition copy and realizing the donation ask
Perhaps the exit intent popup will help make the mind up of anyone that is on the fence about donating
Prompting visitors to second guess their decision to leave the page could lead some to return and convert – but will it be enough to produce a valid lift in conversion?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not produce the results we had hoped for. In fact, it only produced eight conversions in total and they were split between the control and the treatment (exit intent popup). Ultimately, the treatment had a 1.9% lift with only a 2.12% level of confidence.
This approach is worth testing again, but on donation funnel that has ample traffic and overall conversions. We simply don't have enough data from this experiment to prove any effectiveness or lack of effectiveness.

experiments.title: How shorter, more congruent donation page copy impacted donations for a direct ask funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter, more congruent value prop copy will result in an increase in donations because the donor experience will be better-connected and remain on topic as they transition from the direct ask ad to the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
TPPF’s CAF-Focused funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In our analysis, we see that roughly 75% of our traffic for this funnel is coming from mobile and the mobile bounce rate is 95%. In evaluating the user experience, we hypothesize that the donation page copy is too lengthy – especially for mobile users – and that there is a disconnect between the direct ask Facebook ad creative and the instant donation page value proposition copy.
The Facebook ads are generating high click-through rates we believe primarily from the issue-specific yes/no ad graphic in which we ask users if they agree or disagree with a question that is relevant to the opportunity we’re providing to them. The ad copy also speaks specifically about one issue but then speaks much more generally on the donation page.
We need to find a way to maintain the momentum from the ad level to get more people to convert at the donation level.
Shorter copy should better engage our mobile audience and more congruent copy between the ad and the landing page should hone the value proposition copy and better motivate page visitors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment variant with the shorter copy and honed value proposition copy did produce more donations than the control, however, the difference was only one donation. The experiment produced only 5 conversions total: 2 to the control and 3 to the treatment. The 35.3% conversion lift from the treatment variant had only a 26.2% level of confidence.

Simply put, the experiment did not reach the conversion sample to validate one way or the other after running for 30 days. Thus, we cannot make any valid conclusions. We'll keep a look out for other opportunities to launch this type of test.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2023 - 05/29/2023
TPPF’s Pocket Constitution Booklet was getting a ton of traction as an individual content offer. We want to find out if taking that momentum by applying it as a premium as part of a new content offer funnel would produce a lift in donations for the new content offer (School Choice Survey).
We added an image of the Pocket Constitution to the School Choice Survey instant donation page, along with the following language at the end of the value proposition copy to clarify the premium opportunity:
“And if you make a donation of $10 or more today, we will send you a FREE Pocket Constitution for your home or classroom as a reminder of your commitment to better education in the Lone Star State.”
Let’s find out if the added premium offer increases donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test the hypothesis that adding a premium to an instant donation page would increase donations. The treatment included an image of a Pocket Constitution Booklet and an offer to send the booklet to anyone who made a donation of $10 or more on the School Choice Survey instant donation page.

The experiment ran for 32 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 17.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a 22.2% statistical level of confidence.

While the treatment did not produce a bump in donations like we had hoped, it did produce a large bump in revenue through a 645.8% lift with a nearly valid 84.8% level of confidence.

The average gift among donors to the treatment was $90 compared to an average gift of just $10 among those who donated to the control variant.

It seems the extra incentive provided by the Pocket Constitution Booklet premium made people want to give more, even though the minimum donation to receive the premium was $10.

This speaks to the "what do I get in return" mindset many people have. By getting an actual item in return for making a gift, it seems donation page visitors were willing to give more.

The nearly valid increase in revenue gives us the confidence to roll out the treatment, connecting all funnel traffic to the premium opportunity.

experiments.title: How adding statements of credibility to a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility language to the instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain belief in our organization and trust us to make a true impact with their contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 04/13/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis survey offer funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. We’ve run several tests on this page, including a recent test in which we added evidentials to the copy in order to make the problem more tangible and understandable. This test produced a drop in donor conversion rate. The result made us confident that our audience already has a strong understanding of the problem, and that the issue with conversion rate is elsewhere on the page.
So for this experiment, we’re testing adding credibility language with the hypothesis that it will convince our audience that we are capable of solving or improving the problems we face at the border. Perhaps the current donation page does not do a good enough job of separating TPPF from other organizations and selling us as the organization most capable of helping in this critical area.
Our audience understands the problem we’re trying to help solve, but do they trust us and believe that we can solve it?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding credibility language to the instant donation page for potential donors has a significant impact on increasing donations. The treatment group saw a 162.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a 76.6% level of confidence. We saw even greater results in revenue with the treatment producing a 614.2% increase in revenue for all traffic with a valid 96.5% level of statistical confidence. The average gift to the treatment was $20.40 compared to just $7.50 to the control.
Returning visitors converted four times through the treatment compared to zero through the control. This tells us that people who have experience with the organization and have engaged with it in the past appreciate the language separating us from other organizations.
Based on these findings, it is recommended that future experiments focus on improving the credibility and trustworthiness of the organization in order to increase donor conversions. Some future tests could include adding social proof, testimonials, and other forms of credibility language to the donation page.

experiments.title: How “rally together” messaging impacted donations for a recurring giving program
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “rally together” messaging for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will want to unite against the threats to Texas independence and be a part of the efforts to combat those threats.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 02/17/2023
A public policy client has struggled to get traction on their recurring giving donation page. The page is producing a 0.29 instant donor conversion rate heading into this experiment. Assessing the current donation page copy, we wondered if it is too general and does not do a good enough job of inciting emotion among potential donors. We also wondered if page visitors would convert at a higher rate if we apply relevant language to the current public policy landscape in Texas and the threats to the people of the state.
We’ll develop a donation page treatment that uses “rally together” language, highlighting specific threats to Texas and its citizens and making it clear that these threats won’t seize unless we come together as one to fight for better policy and safeguard our personal liberties. The copy will reference current threats and conditions that are close to home and close to the heart of conservative Texans.
We expect this messaging to increase motivation and urgency to join the Come and Take It Society as a monthly donor and make a continuous effort to fight for independence in Texas.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither the control nor the treatment reached significance. The treatment led to 5 donations to 4 for the control. Revenue was a tick higher for the treatment compared to the control, but the increase in revenue only had a 67.1% level of confidence. It seems the "rally together" messaging did not motivate potential donors to convert to donors in the way that we hypothesized it would. The messaging could be worth testing through a regular offer in which someone can make either a one-time or monthly donation. However, for the recurring ask, the messaging did not do enough to make potential donors give recurring gifts at a rate in which we could trust rolling out the treatment.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an incentivized item to an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing an image of the special CATIS coin in the welcome series email for potential CATIS members will achieve an increase in clicks because they’ll be impressed with the coin and gain a true feeling of possessing the coin.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/02/2022
As part of TPPF’s welcome series, new names are sent an invitation email to join the Come and Take It Society (CATIS) by making a monthly gift. When joining, members receive exclusive perks. One perk is receiving a special CATIS coin. This specific perk is mentioned in the email, however, the email does not include an image of the coin to give potential new members an accurate, tangible impression of the coin.
We hypothesized that adding an image of the coin would further pique the interest of email recipients, giving them a preview of the special coin they would receive, and therefore encouraging them to click through the email to the CATIS donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in an 89.9% lift in clicks - a 6.3% click rate compared to 3.3% for the control. Our hypothesis proved correct: Those who saw the coin were more likely to click within the email. The coin image gave these people a preview of the coin and provoked visions of what it would be like to possess the coin. The mere mention of it can only go so far. By just mentioning it, email recipients don't know what the coin looks like and if it'd be worth essentially paying for as part of a monthly donation commitment. They could envision the coin as appearing tacky and poorly made. Showing an image of the coin promotes something tangible and takes some of the guesswork out of the person's mind.

experiments.title: How adding value prop before a petition impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating cognitive momentum with additional value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email signatures.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/16/2020 - 12/09/2020
We were running a petition and were looking for ways to improve email conversion rate on the landing page. Although the Facebook ad gave supporting reasons to sign the petition, we noticed the control landing page went straight into the petition statement without giving any additional context for why someone should add their name to the petition. We wondered if adding clarity and urgency for visitors would help continue the cognitive momentum from one step to the next and developed a treatment that carried over value proposition into a brief paragraph and an additional subheader before leading into the beginning of the petition statement. We split traffic to both versions and ran the test for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the test for 3 weeks, we saw a 1.4% increase in conversion rate with 45.6% level of confidence so the experiment results are not valid. We needed a greater sample size for further indication of whether or not the treatment version was working. Upon review, the new intro paragraph in the treatment used organization-centric language that could have led to flat results. In future tests on this page, we will optimize the copy to be more donor-centric while testing the same concept. We could also implement some design features on the page that more clearly separate the petition language from the introductory value proposition to reduce confusion and friction in the mind of the page visitor.

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a video in an ad for Facebook users will achieve higher click rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
In an effort to increase click through rates on a FB ad promoting a survey, we wanted to know if presenting the issue through video format would provide a lift in click through rates. We hypothesized that by offering a different format that presented the narrative in video form, rather than text, more viewers would stop to interact with the ad and hopefully click through
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we were able to see a 49% lift in click through rates. This indicates that the video format stood out in people's feeds and presented the issue in a way that inspired viewers to click through.

However, due to the low number of impressions received by the two ads, we were unable to validate this experiment. The treatment received only $19.37. We also noted that the video format was not the only ad element that was changed. The body text was also shortened and revised to use more urgent language that compelled viewers to act.

In future experiments, we may want to distribute the spend between control and treatment more evenly and ensure enough spend is designated to provide sufficient traffic. If we can validate the lift provided by the video format, we can then test the copy variations against one another.

experiments.title: How condensed copy affects conversion rates on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing copy on an instant donation page for a survey offer will increase conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2022 - 07/20/2022
We wanted to improve conversion rates on an instant donation page for a survey offer being run by Texas Public Policy Foundation. In order to do so, we decided to test condensed body copy that spends fewer words restating the problem, places a CTA in the headline, and calls attention to the benefits of joining the Come and Take It Society.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 34 days, we observed a 32.8% lift in conversion rates, with a 58.3% level of confidence. An insufficient amount of transactions completed during the testing period prevented us from validating this experiment.

We believe that reducing the copy may have helped provide this lift by becoming more action-oriented, reducing the scroll required to reach the form and by focusing on the impact a donor will have, sooner, while making the CTA clear in the headline.

While we were not able to validate this lift in conversion rates, the results are encouraging. Next, we will want to test different elements on the condensed copy version of this landing page to further this learning and continue to improve results.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a survey confirmation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for survey takers will achieve a lift in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2022 - 06/17/2022
Email conversion rates on a survey offer were performing exceptionally well but the instant donor conversion was sufferably low. We wanted to produce a radical redesign treatment to take advantage of the high traffic coming to the page and see if we could improve conversion. We added a personalized headline, and brought in recency and urgency to the timeline of the issue throughout the body copy. We also introduce the concept of donor autonomy by asking “Would you consider…” and saying “If you make a gift today…” and “Will you join?” Rather than using bullet points to highlight principles of belief like the control, we instead outline the ways their gift will be used in these bullet points demonstrating the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment we observed a 9.8% lift in donations with a 27.5% level of confidence so the test did not validate. When reviewing the results, we hypothesized that both the control and the treatment copy are long and required a lot of scrolling on the page to actually reach the donation form itself that could be causing unnecessary friction. For the next test on this page, we will develop a treatment that has shorter copy and leaves the donation form in view.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a donor countdown impacted donation page visits
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a micro-ask in a sticky bar for website visitors at calendar year-end will achieve an increase in donation page visits.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2021 - 01/01/2022
In order to drive traffic from the website during a high urgency campaign, we launched two versions of a sticky bar to show site visitors. The control used donor alignment and a deadline to indicate urgency. The treatment version used a micro-ask: will you be one of the 10 donors we need today? We ran this as an A/B split test during the month of December to see if we could learn which message would most resonate with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not achieve a significant level of confidence, the experiment ended after 4 weeks with a 5% increase in traffic at a 32% level of confidence for version B. We will need to further test this concept for other campaigns to better understand which message and format will help website visitors take interest in clicking through to the donation page. We will also want to introduce a more specific call to action language on future iterations of this test such as "Click here" to make certain visitors are aware of the next step we would like them to take and how after reading the sticky bar copy.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected housefile engagement for a cultivation email

rows.name: Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing “you” to add relevance in a subject line for a cultivation series to the housefile will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
We were noticing a recent decline in open rate for a weekly cultivation email series to the housefile so we wanted to test some subject line elements to see how we could improve how the audience was responding to different subject lines and increase engagement with the cultivation emails. We established a control—“An interesting interview”—using the element of mystery to invoke curiosity and a treatment—“Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?”—using the word you to create relevancy for the email recipients. We set up the A/B split test to see which of the two elements had better performance so we can continue to adjust subject lines for future emails in the series by using what works for this particular audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was set up in Hubspot to monitor results for the first 4 hours after the email test was sent to 50% of the total audience and then send the email with the most opens to the remainder of the file. Because of this, we did not have a large enough sample size to validate the experiment. The treatment saw a 6.3% decrease in traffic, but we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence to draw any conclusions based on these results. Moving forward, we will want to run a similar test as a split to the full housefile rather than just half of it so we can validate results and find out how to best optimize the subject lines for this audience.

experiments.title: How adding benefits of monthly giving affected clicks in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding benefits of monthly giving for a housefile appeal will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/11/2021
For a high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test if including the benefits of becoming a monthly donor would increase clicks to the donation page. This was the last email in the campaign and it was set up a personal note forwarding the previous email from earlier in the day with one final request to give before the deadline. The treatment version listed the benefits of becoming a monthly donor and included an image of the premium for monthly donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this email was sent to an audience limited in size, we were unable to validate the results of the experiment. We saw a 24.1% increase in traffic on the treatment but with only a 43.8% level of confidence so we cannot claim statistical significance. Moving forward, we'll plan to retest this same concept in future emails—as well as launch the test in other channels that may reach a wider audience —to see if there are any learnings to be applied.

experiments.title: How including a video thumbnail affects clicks in a cultivation email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video thumbnail outperform a raw link in number of clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/04/2020
TPPF wanted to test if including a video thumbnail would increase the number of clicks in a cultivation email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, this experiment did not get enough traffic to reach the level of confidence needed to validate.

experiments.title: How more direct copy affected donations on a landing page

rows.name: Variant 1 -- copy test, more direct copy

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct headline that tells people what they just did (and more direct body copy) increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2019 - 08/26/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation sent chase emails to their file for a direct mail letter about ending the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare). They then versioned those chase emails to send to the rest of their file. They wanted to test the donation page copy to see if a more direct headline and explanation of what TPPF is doing to combat Obamacare would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: This copy experiment led to a 27.1% decrease in donations. However, there are several factors in the data that make these experiment results inconclusive. The sample size is too small, there is only a one gift difference, and the level of confidence is too low to come close to validating. Given this information, the experiment results cannot be used for future communications to the TPPF donor file.

experiments.title: How deploying a coaster premium affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of a premium incentivize more people to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2019 - 06/28/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation ran a campaign to fund a legal case to defend John Yearwood against the federal government. They had a coaster premium emblazoned with Texas’ famous motto “Come and Take It” that they had used in direct mail, and wanted to test it on the donation page to see if it would increase conversion. They created a simple treatment that added an image of the coaster to the page and split traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than two months, the coaster premium showed a directional decline in donations, though it wasn’t statistically valid and did not reach a valid sample size. Since this wasn’t able to move the needle, TPPF decided to go back to the drawing board and find a new test to deliver a radical result.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a main donation page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding third-party validation and more context about the organization in the copy increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) is testing their donation pages to increase conversion rates. They wondered if talking more about Texas and how TPPF can help Texans and the rest of America be more free would make a bigger impact on donations. They decided to take a radical redesign approach to their main donation page. They added a header with a call-to-action, more copy that explained what TPPF does and who they help, removed links that took people to a different page, and added an image and quote of a famous Texan for third-party validation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results from this experiment were surprising. The radical redesign caused a directional 38.4% decrease in revenue, but with no statistical validity. The original donation page had a better conversion rate, even with the less-inclusive copy and the links to another site. However, because the confidence level is not at or above 95%, this experiment is not statistically valid. TPPF should continue to run experiments on their main donation page, but overall conversions combined will make it difficult to validate a result.

experiments.title: How adding third-party validation to a donation page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a quote and image of a famed Texan increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) ran a petition in support of John Yearwood, a veteran who is working with TPPF to defend his land from the government. After the petition, petition signers have an opportunity to make a donation in support of TPPF and people like John Yearwood. TPPF wanted to run an experiment on the donation page where they tested the effect of third-party validation on conversion rates. They decided to use a quote and image of former Texas governor Rick Perry to see if it would lift conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The conversion rates for each of the two donation page versions during the one month that this experiment ran were the virtually the same, which meant that the photo alone had no impact on conversion rate. This means that TPPF must make bigger changes to impact conversion in this funnel.

experiments.title: How validating shared beliefs affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a section to validate shared beliefs affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a campaign to raise support for their Center for the American Future, which defended citizens against government overreach. They were running a petition of support for John Yearwood, a rancher who faced a threat from the government if he disturbed the habitat of a rare spider—one which he had never seen!
They ran an experiment on the petition page to see if adding a section that asked the reader to confirm their shared values—namely that Texas landowners should be able to use their land as they please, that the federal government should let property owners conserve their own land, and that the government should follow the 10th Amendment—would increase email acquisition. Previous testing had implied that these checkboxes could have an impact on email acquisition (more people would sign the petition) and donations (more people would give after signing). They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the shared values checkboxes had no effect on email acquisition. However, the downstream data must be examined further to understand how this is affecting follow-on conversion.

experiments.title: How adding background information in the copy affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a storyline and more information about what Mr. John Yearwood is up against increase giving?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2018
The Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a petition on behalf of a Texas citizen who was under threat of legal action from the federal government. After the petition, signers were given the opportunity to make a donation to this man’s legal fund. Donor conversion was below their benchmark, so they decided to run an experiment. The control copy assumes the reader knows the backstory of Mr. Yearwood. They hypothesized that because this case was not well known, that potential donors might need more  background information, including more on the challenges facing Mr. Yearwood. They created a treatment that ended up being quite long and included a thermometer to show progress and social proof. They launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 11 donations to the control and 12 donations to the treatment, they found that there was no difference in conversion between the two pages. While the sample size is not large enough to validate this experiment, current results indicate that even with more traffic, it is likely that there still won't be significant difference in donations between the two experiments.
This prompted them to run another experiment that heightened urgency to see if they could move the needle on donor conversion.
